title: Team Development & Leadership
summary: How project managers build, motivate, and lead teams through predictable development stages and conflicts. Framed for PMBOK 8: team development and management is the Lead the Team process within the Resources performance domain, supported by the Build an Empowered Culture and Be an Accountable Leader principles - where leadership is a behaviour anyone can demonstrate, not a title.
intro: Grow a team through Tuckman, lead by behaviour, and handle conflict the PMP way.

sections.heading: Where this fits
sections.body: In PMBOK 8 the people work lives in the Resources performance domain - team development and management is the Lead the Team process - and is driven by two principles: Build an Empowered Culture, and Be an Accountable Leader.

blocks.title: How to read this guide
lines: Resources performance domain = the outcome: a high-performing team with shared ownership (team dev/mgmt = the Lead the Team process).
Build an Empowered Culture = create a collaborative, accountable team environment.
Be an Accountable Leader = leadership is a behaviour, not a title; anyone can show it.
Tailor your style to the team's maturity and the situation - no single style always wins.

sections.heading: Tuckman's stages of team development
sections.body: Teams mature through predictable stages. As they do, the PM shifts leadership style from directing to coaching to facilitating to delegating. Stages are not strictly one-way: a change such as a new member, a new leader or a major scope shift can send a settled team back to storming.

items.title: Forming
lines: Polite, uncertain, dependent on the leader for direction.
PM directs: set goals, roles and ground rules.

items.title: Storming
lines: Friction surfaces as members assert opinions; conflict over approach is common.
PM coaches: surface issues, build trust, keep things moving.

items.title: Norming
lines: Norms and working agreements settle; cooperation and cohesion grow.
PM facilitates: support collaboration, step back from control.

items.title: Performing
lines: High-trust, self-organising, focused on results with minimal oversight.
PM delegates: empower the team, remove impediments.

items.title: Adjourning (mourning)
lines: Work completes and the team disbands.
Recognise contributions and capture lessons learned.

blocks.title: Lead each stage differently
blocks.note: Teams can slip back a stage when membership or context changes - re-read the stage and adjust your style rather than assuming steady progress.

rows.value: Directing - give clear direction, goals, and structure.
Coaching - mediate, clarify roles, and keep the goal visible.
Supporting - share decisions and reinforce working agreements.
Delegating - step back, let the team self-manage, and clear blockers.
Releasing - recognise contribution, capture lessons, and transition people well.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: Forming-Storming-Norming-Performing-Adjourning. Leadership goes directing -> coaching -> facilitating -> delegating. A big change can reset a team to storming.

sections.heading: Conflict resolution modes (Thomas-Kilmann)
sections.body: PMI recognises five ways to handle conflict; the right one depends on the situation. Collaborate is win-win and PMI's preferred mode.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 210' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='48' y1='14' x2='48' y2='174' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='48' y1='174' x2='304' y2='174' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><circle cx='90' cy='48' r='3.5' fill='#B5462F'/><text x='90' y='40' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Force</text><circle cx='258' cy='48' r='3.5' fill='#2F6F5E'/><text x='258' y='40' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Collaborate</text><circle cx='174' cy='96' r='3.5' fill='#1F5FA8'/><text x='174' y='88' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Compromise</text><circle cx='90' cy='150' r='3.5' fill='currentColor'/><text x='90' y='166' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Withdraw</text><circle cx='258' cy='150' r='3.5' fill='currentColor'/><text x='258' y='166' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Smooth</text><text x='30' y='94' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 30 94)'>Assertiveness</text><text x='176' y='196' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Cooperativeness</text></svg>
figure.caption: The five modes by assertiveness vs cooperativeness; Collaborate (top-right) is win-win.

rows.value: Work the problem together for a win-win that incorporates multiple viewpoints. PMI's preferred mode and the best lasting outcome.
Give-and-take so each side gets partial satisfaction; quick but often lose-lose where no one is fully happy.
Emphasise areas of agreement and yield your position to keep harmony; only a temporary fix.
Push your own view at others' expense (win-lose); fast but breeds resentment, so reserve for emergencies or safety.
Retreat from or postpone the issue; not a true resolution.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: Collaborate/confront is always the best answer unless time, stakes or safety force otherwise. Picture the five modes on assertiveness (up) vs cooperativeness (right); Collaborate sits top-right as win-win.

sections.heading: Sources of conflict
sections.body: Conflict is natural and inevitable on projects, and it is best resolved openly and directly by the people involved, with the PM facilitating rather than imposing. The classic ranking of conflict causes runs from most to least common - and personality clashes sit last, not first. Most conflict is driven by the work and how it is organised, not by people simply disliking each other.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: Schedules cause the most conflict; personality the least. Resolve early, openly, by those involved.

sections.heading: Motivation theories
sections.body: Several models explain what drives people; the exam expects you to recognise each.

rows.value: Needs are met bottom-up: physiological -> safety -> social/belonging -> esteem -> self-actualisation. Lower needs must be reasonably satisfied before higher ones motivate.
Hygiene factors (salary, working conditions, policies) only prevent dissatisfaction; they do not motivate. True motivators are achievement, recognition, responsibility and growth.
Theory X assumes people dislike work and need control and supervision; Theory Y assumes they are self-motivated and seek responsibility.
People are driven mainly by achievement, affiliation or power; match work to a person's dominant need.

items.text: Ouchi's model: long-term employment, consensus decisions, and whole-person care build the loyalty that sustains Theory Y.
People improve when they know they are watched; attention can motivate, but metrics under scrutiny can be gamed - read the data with care.

blocks.title: Herzberg trap
blocks.text: A pay rise removes dissatisfaction but does not motivate. Recognition and growth do.

sections.heading: Power & influence
sections.body: A PM's authority comes from several sources of power. Expert and referent power are generally the most effective and are earned over time, so favour them. Coercive power is the least effective and should be a last resort.

groups.label: Expert
lines: Influence earned through knowledge, skill and credibility.

groups.label: Referent
lines: Influence from respect, charisma or association with someone respected.

groups.label: Formal / positional
lines: Authority from the role or title.

groups.label: Reward
lines: Ability to give bonuses, recognition or desirable assignments.

groups.label: Coercive / penalty
lines: Ability to punish or threaten consequences. Least effective - last resort.

blocks.title: Cialdini's six levers of influence
blocks.note: Use these to earn genuine agreement, not to pressure - influence, not manipulation.

items.text: People repay favours - give help and value first.
People honour prior public agreements - secure small, visible commitments.
People follow peers - show that similar teams already do it.
People defer to credible experts - bring in the right sponsor or specialist.
People say yes to those they trust - build rapport and listen.
People value what is limited - be honest about real time or resource constraints.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: Earned beats granted: expert and referent are best; coercive/penalty is last resort.

sections.heading: Leadership styles
sections.body: PMBOK 8 treats leadership as a behaviour anyone can demonstrate, not a title. Match the style to the team's maturity and the situation - the agile default is servant leadership.

rows.value: Agile default; serve the team and remove impediments.
Crisis, or a new / inexperienced team needing structure.
Match style to team maturity - the through-line of Tuckman.
Rewards for performance against clear targets.
Vision and change energy; inspire beyond self-interest.
Hands-off; works for an expert, self-directed team.

blocks.title: Cue
blocks.text: Agile -> servant leadership. Leadership is a behaviour, not just a title, and the best style depends on team maturity and context.

sections.heading: Psychological safety
sections.body:
Psychological safety is a shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking. People can raise concerns, admit mistakes, ask questions, and propose imperfect ideas without fear of blame or ridicule.

Without safety, problems stay hidden, learning slows, and conflict goes underground. With it, teams surface issues early, experiment, and improve. Safety is not the absence of standards; it is the presence of respect while holding high expectations for the work.

Leaders build safety by modeling curiosity, separating people from problems, protecting confidentiality when appropriate, and discouraging personal attacks. They also close the loop: when someone speaks up, acknowledge it and show what changed.
sections.tip: On the exam, if fear of speaking up is the root issue, fix the environment before adding process. Safety enables honest status, risk reporting, and healthy conflict.

groups.label: What
lines: People share half-formed thoughts and dissenting views.

groups.label: Use
lines: Better options surface earlier; groupthink drops.

groups.label: What
lines: Bad news and uncertainty are reported promptly.

groups.label: Use
lines: You can respond while options remain open.

groups.label: What
lines: Mistakes are treated as data, not as character flaws.

groups.label: Use
lines: Retrospectives and continuous improvement become real, not performative.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Safety supports empowerment. A self-organizing team cannot thrive if members are punished for visible learning or dissent.

sections.heading: Active listening and collaborative conversations
sections.body:
Active listening means giving full attention, seeking to understand before you respond, and reflecting back meaning so the speaker feels heard. It is not waiting for your turn to talk. In conflict and coaching, it lowers heat and surfaces real interests behind positions.

Collaborative conversations are open, multi-directional exchanges grounded in mutual respect. Everyone can contribute; ideas are challenged without attacking people. The leader's job is to invite quieter voices, keep discussion on the problem, and prevent dominance by a few speakers.

Assertive communication complements both skills: state needs, facts, and boundaries clearly without dismissing others. Focus on the issue and impact, not on blame.
sections.tip: If a scenario shows misunderstanding or escalating emotion, choose listening and clarifying before deciding or directing. Heard people de-escalate faster.

items.title: Focus and hold judgment
lines: Give undivided attention; notice content and emotion.
Avoid interrupting with solutions or defenses.

items.title: Reflect and check
lines: Paraphrase: What I am hearing is... Is that right?
Name feelings carefully when they matter to the issue.

items.title: Respond with intent
lines: Ask clarifying questions; confirm interests and constraints.
Then move to options, decisions, or next steps.

blocks.title: Conversation habits that help or hurt

rows.value: Invite all voices, use I statements, separate people from problems, seek common ground.
Talk over others, personalize criticism, debate to win status, or punish candor.

sections.heading: Coaching, mentoring, and teaching
sections.body:
Developing people is a core leadership behavior, not a side activity. Teaching transfers knowledge and skills. Coaching draws out the person's own thinking so they grow judgment and ownership. Mentoring shares experience and perspective over a longer arc of growth.

On projects, use teaching when a skill gap is clear and time-sensitive. Use coaching when the person can reason through options with support. Use mentoring when career context, political navigation, or long-term capability matters. In adaptive teams, leaders often coach more and dictate less so the team builds self-reliance.

Effective development is goal-linked: identify strengths and gaps, set realistic improvement goals, practice, and follow up with feedback. The aim is capability the team can apply, not training theater.
sections.tip: Exam cue: if the issue is growth and performance, coach or mentor. If the issue is missing knowledge for a task, teach. If the issue is blocked work, remove impediments first.

groups.label: What
lines: Explain concepts, techniques, or standards so others can apply them.

groups.label: Use
lines: New tools, process rules, domain facts, or lessons from prior work.

groups.label: What
lines: Guide reflection, goals, and practice; the coachee owns the solution.

groups.label: Use
lines: Performance growth, problem-solving skill, and confidence under change.

groups.label: What
lines: Offer experience-based guidance and broader career or organizational context.

groups.label: Use
lines: Longer-term development, navigation of culture, and professional judgment.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Servant leaders develop people so the team can deliver without constant direction. Coaching supports self-organization; micromanaging undermines it.

sections.heading: Empowerment and self-organization
sections.body:
Empowerment means people have the authority, information, tools, and support to make decisions within clear boundaries. Self-organization is the team's ability to plan work, solve problems, and adjust how they collaborate without waiting for constant instruction.

Leaders set purpose, constraints, and success criteria, then step back from task-level control. They still provide guardrails: vision, priorities, definitions of done, and escalation paths. Autonomy without clarity becomes chaos; control without trust becomes bottleneck.

Empowered teams show higher ownership, faster local decisions, and better creativity. The leader's leverage shifts to removing impediments, protecting focus, and facilitating agreement when the team is stuck.
sections.tip: PMI favors empowering the team. If a question offers take over vs enable the team with clear goals and support, enable is usually correct unless safety or crisis requires directive action.

items.title: Clarify outcomes and limits
lines: Define goals, priorities, quality bars, and decision rights.
Make constraints visible: budget, compliance, interfaces, and deadlines.

items.title: Delegate real authority
lines: Let the team choose how to approach work inside the guardrails.
Avoid re-deciding every local choice.

items.title: Support and unblock
lines: Provide resources, coaching, and protection from thrash.
Remove impediments and escalate organizational barriers the team cannot fix alone.

rows.value: Clear purpose, shared norms, available support, and accountable ownership.
Hands-off without goals, feedback, or help when the team is blocked.
Constant task control that signals distrust and slows delivery.

sections.heading: Feedback and facilitating performance
sections.body:
Feedback is timely information about behavior and results that helps people improve. It should be specific, constructive, and balanced: recognize what works, name gaps with evidence, and agree on next actions. In adaptive work, short feedback loops (reviews, demos, retrospectives, one-on-ones) keep learning continuous.

Facilitating performance means making high performance easier: clear goals, the right tools, removed obstacles, and fair accountability. Leaders notice strengths and stretch assignments, and they do not confuse busy activity with outcomes.

Emotional intelligence strengthens both feedback and facilitation. Self-awareness and self-regulation keep your delivery calm. Empathy helps you tailor the message. Social skill helps you read the room and keep relationships intact while raising standards.
sections.tip: Prefer timely, private, behavior-based feedback over delayed personality judgments. Public praise can motivate; public shaming rarely improves performance.

items.text: Cite observable work or behavior, not vague labels.
Give feedback close to the event while it is still actionable.
Pair the observation with a clear improvement or next experiment.
Invite the other person's view; feedback is a conversation, not a monologue.

groups.label: What
lines: Shared definition of success and priorities.

groups.label: Use
lines: Align effort and make trade-offs explicit.

groups.label: What
lines: Eliminate blockers, unclear handoffs, and needless process.

groups.label: Use
lines: Protect flow so talent can convert into results.

groups.label: What
lines: Visible commitments, reviews, and fair consequences.

groups.label: Use
lines: Turn good intentions into sustained delivery.

blocks.title: If you must remove a team member

rows.value: Record the specific performance or conduct issue with facts and dates, not opinions.
Give feedback, coaching, and a fair chance to improve - unless it is a serious ethics or safety breach.
Work with HR and the resource or functional manager; respect contracts and organisational process.
Notify affected stakeholders and plan knowledge transfer to limit disruption.
Adjust the resource plan, RACI, and risk register, and capture the lesson learned.

sections.heading: Conflict process toolkit
sections.body:
Beyond choosing a conflict mode, project leaders use process tools to understand and resolve disputes. Start with conflict analysis: who is involved, what interests sit under the positions, what triggers raised intensity, and what outcomes each party needs. Then choose an intervention that matches severity and relationship risk.

Early intervention and de-escalation keep conflict productive. When emotions run high, slow the conversation, restate interests, and re-center on shared goals. Collaborative problem-solving seeks an inclusive solution that addresses root causes, not only a quick split of positions.

When parties cannot resolve it alone, a neutral third party may help. Facilitation structures dialogue toward consensus. Mediation guides parties to their own agreement. Negotiation is direct bargaining for a mutually acceptable deal. Arbitration hands the decision to a third party and is more formal, used when other approaches fail or a binding call is required.
sections.tip: Default path on the exam: address conflict early, with those involved, aiming for a collaborative solution. Escalate process (mediate, then more formal steps) only when needed. Avoid jumping to force or arbitration as the first move.

rows.value: Neutral guide structures discussion so all voices are heard and the group reaches agreement.
Parties bargain directly to reconcile interests; success depends on communication and trade-offs.
Neutral third party helps parties craft their own resolution; the mediator does not impose the answer.
Neutral third party decides the outcome; more formal and often used after other methods fail.

items.title: Analyze before you prescribe
lines: Map stakeholders, interests, facts, and escalation signals.
Separate symptoms (anger, silence) from causes (schedule pressure, unclear roles).

items.title: De-escalate and open dialogue
lines: Create a safe space; use active listening and assertive issue framing.
Keep focus on the problem and desired outcomes.

items.title: Solve or escalate the process
lines: Seek a collaborative solution the parties own.
If stuck, use facilitation or mediation; reserve formal third-party decision for last-resort cases.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Collaborate/problem-solve is the preferred mode; mediation and facilitation are process supports for that outcome. Arbitration is decisive but not automatically win-win.

source: PM Cram — aligned to the current PMBOK Guide and PMI Examination Content Outline.
